dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Hey everybody, it's Haitch and welcome to the latest installment of Dune Pod, your one stop shop to get ready for the new Dune movie, or just a fun place to talk movies if you don't care about Dune. This week I'm joined as always by my co-host Jason.

dialogues.name: JASON
dialogues.text: Yes, that's what I want for this relationship is hatred and awkwardness.

dialogues.text: And by the founder and CEO of Warhorn, Brian Moseley.

dialogues.name: BRIAN I
dialogues.text: don't want to have to read the script of every single movie in order to enjoy it.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: On this episode, we discussed the epic sword and sorcery movie that launched Gurney Halleck himself, Sir Patrick Stewart's career, 1981's Excalibur. We discuss the power of a majestic score, the dangers of directors casting their own children in their movies, and recite the charm of making. If you're enjoying the show, we need your help. Be cool, like Seth410 and leave us a five star rating and a review wherever you listen to your podcasts as it really helps new listeners find the show. And now without further a-Dune, Excalibur. [Dune Pod theme music fades out]

dialogues.text: Well, I got really into tournament Dungeons and Dragons back in like 2001. And you'll remember, I dragged you in many of our friends into it as well. And we would go all over the Bay Area, and sometimes the country to tournaments.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: And sometimes the world.

dialogues.name: BRIAN
dialogues.text: Sometimes the world. And so I started running my own tournaments. So I built some software, a website to organize it all, put it all online, and 20 years later, it's still running. And there's a couple 100,000 users and it's sort of a big deal in this very tiny little segment of the tabletop role playing game hobby space.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: Amazing, amazing. And Jason, just so you know that there was this campaign, it was called Living Greyhawk. It was done by Wizards of the Coast, which is the company that makes D&D, and they had adventures that could literally only be run adventures in the campaign world, this fantasy world of Greyhawk that could only be run within certain maps on the real world. So for instance, a bunch of adventures could only be run in California, other adventures could only be run in Illinois. So if you wanted to go and play a certain adventure, you'd have to go fly to that location and find a DM that was willing to run it for you.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH
dialogues.text: So, in the olden days back in the 70s, when the original, you know, AD&D, or before even AD&D, when the original D&D folks were gathering together, they actually did kind of classical tournament style gaming, where there was points scored and I think like different tables are playing the same adventure and trying to get through it faster with your deaths or whatever. But by the time Matt and I got involved in it, it was just it was more of this like living campaign, you make a character, you bring it to game after game, and they level and get XP and magic items and stuff.

dialogues.name: MATT HAITCH

Arthur, a lowly Squire learns his true heritage as the rightful king when he does what no one else could, drawing the sword Excalibur from the stone where it has lain trapped for a generation. Under the guidance of Merlin the Magician, Arthur will forge a nation calling Knights to join his roundtable at Camelot. But when the bravest and most noble of them all, Lancelot, betrays Arthur by laying with his queen, the irresistible Guinevere, the land is shattered once again. Will Arthur's Knights complete their quests, find the holy grail, and restore Arthur and the land?
